consistent_failures_without_skill.impact: Generic openers ('Welcome to X') delay the reader from understanding what the tool does. First impressions matter.
consistent_failures_without_skill.skill_fix: SKILL.md rule: 'Never start with Welcome to, Introduction, or About sections. Start with what it does.'

consistent_failures_without_skill.impact: Explanation buried in '## What is snapenv?' — user must scroll to understand purpose.
consistent_failures_without_skill.skill_fix: SKILL.md: 'First paragraph is the elevator pitch. One or two sentences max.'

consistent_failures_without_skill.impact: '## Troubleshooting' with one sentence and '## Related Projects' as a stub signal an incomplete doc and waste reader time.
consistent_failures_without_skill.skill_fix: SKILL.md: 'Never include a section you cannot fill with real content right now.'

consistent_failures_without_skill.impact: Vague feature lists ('Easy to use, Production ready') convey no information and erode trust in the documentation.
consistent_failures_without_skill.skill_fix: SKILL.md: 'Replace feature bullet lists with concrete examples. Show, don't tell.'

consistent_failures_without_skill.impact: A rewrite that adds length without adding value has failed its purpose. Without-skill response grew from 60 to 65 lines.
consistent_failures_without_skill.skill_fix: SKILL.md: 'If in doubt, cut. Shorter is almost always better. Every line must earn its place.'

consistent_failures_without_skill.impact: No limitations section leaves users to discover constraints at runtime. Dishonest by omission.
consistent_failures_without_skill.skill_fix: SKILL.md: 'Document what the tool cannot do. Limitations sections build trust.'

consistent_failures_without_skill.impact: CONTRIBUTING.md at 66 lines for a solo tool is overengineered and discourages contributors.
consistent_failures_without_skill.skill_fix: SKILL.md: 'CONTRIBUTING.md for small projects should be 30-60 lines. Solo maintainer = fewer process steps.'

consistent_failures_without_skill.impact: 'Our community follows a Code of Conduct' boilerplate on a solo CLI tool signals copy-paste docs, not genuine community guidelines.
consistent_failures_without_skill.skill_fix: SKILL.md: 'Skip CoC boilerplate unless you actually have a community. For solo tools, one sentence on tone suffices.'

consistent_failures_without_skill.impact: Corporate tone ('We welcome contributions from the community!') feels impersonal for a one-person Go tool and signals auto-generated docs.
consistent_failures_without_skill.skill_fix: SKILL.md: 'Write like a human. Use first person. Skip the royal 'we' on solo projects.'

skill_strengths: WITH_SKILL achieves 100% pass rate (17/17) across all three evals
WITH_SKILL consistently avoids welcome/intro fluff in all scenarios
WITH_SKILL produces honest Limitations sections (Eval 1: single-machine, <1GB data)
WITH_SKILL calibrates tone to context: solo tool = direct, personal, no corporate boilerplate
WITH_SKILL line counts stay well under limits (60 lines README, 46 lines CONTRIBUTING)
WITH_SKILL rewrites actually shorten content while adding quality (55 lines vs 65 for rewrite)
without_skill_weaknesses: Average 46.7% pass rate (8/17 assertions) — consistent failure on style/tone assertions
Without-skill defaults to 'corporate template' documentation: Welcome opener, feature lists, CoC boilerplate
Without-skill does produce working content (installation, commands, PR steps) — technical facts are correct
Without-skill fails on: brevity, honest limitations, tone calibration, and removing filler
The gap is not about knowledge — it's about judgment: knowing WHAT to omit is the skill
gap_analysis.key_insight: The skill teaches a documentation philosophy: documentation exists to serve the reader, not the author. WITH_SKILL responses show this — they cut filler, lead with value, and calibrate length and tone to the actual project context. WITHOUT_SKILL defaults to a corporate template that wastes reader time regardless of the project's actual nature.
improvement_areas_for_skill: Could add explicit examples of bad vs good openings (Welcome to X → tool does Y in one sentence)
Could strengthen guidance on calibrating doc length to project scale (solo vs team vs enterprise)
Could add a checklist: 'Before publishing, remove any section that says TBD, TODO, or has fewer than 3 real sentences'
SKILL.md is already concise (227 lines) — no major structural changes needed

notes: ANALYST: pragmatic-docs skill achieves 100% pass rate (17/17) vs 46.7% without skill — a +53.3pp improvement.
ANALYST: No bugs found in the skill. All instructions are correct and produce the right behavior.
ANALYST: The skill's value is in teaching judgment about what to omit. Technical content (commands, installation, PR steps) is correct in both configurations.

ANALYST: The largest gap is in Eval 2 (CONTRIBUTING.md): without-skill scores 40% due to CoC boilerplate, corporate tone, and length. With-skill scores 100% by calibrating to the solo maintainer context.
ANALYST: Eval 0 and 1 each show 50% without-skill (3/6 assertions) — without-skill gets facts right but fails on style, brevity, and presentation.
ANALYST: Improvement suggestion: add an explicit 'anti-pattern' table to SKILL.md — a before/after showing exactly which phrases to eliminate. Currently the guidance is implicit.
ANALYST: The skill is production-ready. Recommend closing issue #33 with status: complete, no fixes required.
